topics: 🏹 R' Yochanan: dog with pitta is כְּזוֹרֵק אֶת הַחֵץ — like a thrown arrow
🐂 Tam vs. Mu'ad: R' Yehuda vs. R' Meir on the threshold
🩸 Niddah parallel: same machloket about gaps in evidence
⚖️ Halakha: R' Meir for tam, R' Yehuda for testimony (הַעֲדָאָה)
👤 אָדָם מוּעָד לְעוֹלָם; opening of perek 3 (הַמַּנִּיחַ אֶת הַכַּד)

overview.title: 📖 Overview — A Long, Layered Daf
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: Daf 11 is dense: nine segments stretching across three Mishnayot. The opening unpacks the dog-with-hot-pitta case — Resh Lakish wants ignition contact at every sheaf; R' Yochanan rules נַעֲשֶׂה כְּזוֹרֵק אֶת הַחֵץ, and supporting beraitot test this against capital-vs-monetary liability. The middle of the daf moves to the tam/mu'ad Mishnah — R' Yehuda demands three days of goring, R' Meir three episodes — and the Yerushalmi explores what counts as continuity, paralleling a niddah dispute between Rav Ada bar Aḥawa and Rav Huna. Then comes the famous אָדָם מוּעָד לְעוֹלָם, with R' Yitzchak's narrowing: only when both were already sleeping. Finally the daf opens perek 3 with הַמַּנִּיחַ אֶת הַכַּד.
overview.hebrewSource: רִבִּי יוֹחָנָן אָמַר. נַעֲשֶׂה כְּזוֹרֵק אֶת הַחֵץ מִמָּקוֹם לְמָקוֹם

slides.title: 💬 The Dog and the Burning Sheaves
slides.subtitle: Why is full damage paid for the pitta but only half for the stack?

lines.speaker: RL
lines.text: בְּמַצִּית אֶת הָאוֹר עַל כָּל שִׁיבּוֹלֶת וְשִׁיבּוֹלֶת — only if the dog rekindled the fire on every single ear of grain.

lines.speaker: R' Yo
lines.text: נַעֲשֶׂה כְּזוֹרֵק אֶת הַחֵץ מִמָּקוֹם לְמָקוֹם — once the dog set the first ear ablaze, it is like an arrow shot from one place to another. The whole stack is treated as one act.

lines.speaker: R' Yitz. b. Tev.
lines.text: A beraita supports Resh Lakish: a kid bound to it, slave nearby = liable; slave bound, kid nearby = exempt. If R' Yochanan's arrow theory were right, the first ear would carry capital liability — and the rest would still be monetary, contradicting the beraita.

lines.speaker: R' Yose
lines.text: Same point from Shabbat: an ox lighting a stack on Shabbat = its owner pays; a person lighting it on Shabbat = exempt (because of capital liability). Without the arrow theory, monetary liability for later sheaves would still attach.

callout.label: Yerushalmi style:
callout.text: The Yerushalmi probes the arrow-thrower theory by lining it up against capital-vs-monetary clashes. If the first ignition triggers a death penalty, all subsequent damage is absorbed into the original capital act.

slides.title: 📊 Tam vs. Mu'ad — R' Yehuda and R' Meir
slides.subtitle: What gets a bull declared mu'ad, and what brings him back to tam?
rows: When children play with it (תִּינּוֹקוֹת מְמַשְׁמְשִׁין בּוֹ)
Spaced gorings: must be three consecutive days

callout.label: Halakhic ruling:
callout.text: Rav Yirmiyah in Rav's name: הֲלָכָה כְרִבִּי מֵאִיר בְּתַמָּה וּכְרִבִּי יוּדָה בְהַעֲדָאָה. Practice splits the dispute — R' Meir governs the threshold for tam, R' Yehuda governs the standard of testimony.

slides.title: 🐂 The Gap-Day Question
slides.subtitle: If a bull gored, then skipped a day, then gored again — what is its status?

nodes.content:
Day 1: gored. Day 2: not let out. Day 3: let out and gored.
Mu'ad or not?

Yerushalmi: יָיבֹא כְתַפְלוּגְתָא דְּרַב אָדָא בַּר אַחֲוָוא וּדְרַב הוּנָא
Treat it as the niddah dispute

columns.label: Rav Ada bar Aḥawa (in Rav's name)
columns.content:
נִידָּה וַודַּאי
Definitely niddah → analogously, definitely mu'ad

nodes.content: Rav Huna: I was there at the start, the middle, and the end — Rav reversed himself, ending on סָפֵק. Rav Ada only heard the first answer (וַודַּאי).

callout.label: Why the niddah parallel:
callout.text: Both cases ask: when there is a gap-day in the middle, do we treat the bookend evidence as a continuous record (וַודַּאי) or as broken (סָפֵק)? It is the same structural question, transferred from blood to goring.

slides.title: 👤 אָדָם מוּעָד לְעוֹלָם — The Mishnah's Sweep
slides.subtitle: And R' Yitzchak's surprising narrowing.

steps.content: 1️⃣ Mishnah: a person is always notorious — shogeg or mezid, awake or asleep.
2️⃣ If he blinded another or broke vessels, he pays full damages.
3️⃣ R' Yitzchak: מַתְנִיתָא בְּשֶׁהָיוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם יְשֵׁינִין — the Mishnah is when both were already sleeping side by side.
4️⃣ But if one was sleeping first and the other came and lay down beside him, the incoming sleeper is the muad — he created the danger.
5️⃣ Net rule: 'always muad' is broad, but liability shifts to whoever changed the situation.

callout.label: Bottom line:
callout.text: Even אָדָם מוּעָד לְעוֹלָם has a structural ceiling: when one person joins another already at rest, the joining party owns the risk.

slides.title: 🏺 Closing the Daf — The Jar in the Public Domain (3:1)
slides.subtitle: The daf opens perek 3 with a paradigmatic case.

boxes.title: 🚶 The walker stumbles
items: He breaks the jar by tripping → exempt
He cannot be sued for damaging the obstacle

boxes.title: 🤕 The walker is hurt
items: וְאִם הוּזַּק בָּהּ בַּעַל הֶחָבִית חַיָיב בִּנְזָקָיו
Jar-owner pays for the walker's injury
He created a hazard in the public domain

boxes.title: 🪨 Compare: hot water bottle case
items: Earlier in the daf: a man set out a פֻּלַטֵירָה in the open
A donkey passed and broke it
R' Yitzchak b. Tevelai: לֹא חַיָיב לָךְ כְּלוּם — and if the donkey was hurt, he pays!

callout.label: The shared logic:
callout.text: Whoever places an object in the public domain bears the risk of what happens to it and what it does to others. The Mishnah of הַמַּנִּיחַ אֶת הַכַּד codifies what R' Yitzchak b. Tevelai already ruled in the practical case.

summary.principle: From a dog with a pitta to a sleeping man and a forgotten jar — daf 11 maps how liability flows when actions ripple, when patterns form, and when bystanders bear the cost.

flowSteps.content: The dog and the stack: split payment — full for pitta, half for stack
Resh Lakish: every-sheaf ignition / R' Yochanan: כְּזוֹרֵק אֶת הַחֵץ

columns.content:
🐂 Tam → mu'ad
R' Yehuda: 3 days · R' Meir: 3 episodes

🩸 Niddah parallel
וודאי or ספק when day 2 is missing

flowSteps.content:
🎓 Key Principle:
Liability is built around the moment of choice: who lit the first ear, who joined the sleeping one, who set the jar down. Halakha picks R' Meir's threshold for tam and R' Yehuda's standard for testimony.

quiz.q: How does R' Yochanan explain the dog-with-pitta case?
o: Only if it lit every single ear of grain
It is like a thrown arrow (כְּזוֹרֵק אֶת הַחֵץ מִמָּקוֹם לְמָקוֹם)
Only if the dog is muad
The owner is exempt entirely
quiz.e: R' Yochanan treats the original ignition as a single act that propagates, like an arrow. Resh Lakish demands per-sheaf rekindling.

quiz.q: What is the practical halakha that Rav Yirmiyah reports in Rav's name about tam/mu'ad?
o: Always like R' Yehuda
Always like R' Meir
הֲלָכָה כְרִבִּי מֵאִיר בְּתַמָּה וּכְרִבִּי יוּדָה בְהַעֲדָאָה
הֲלָכָה כְרִבִּי יוּדָה בְּתַמָּה וּכְרִבִּי מֵאִיר בְהַעֲדָאָה
quiz.e: Practice follows R' Meir for the threshold of tam (three episodes) and R' Yehuda for testimony / he'adah (three days).

quiz.q: What halakhic dispute does the Yerushalmi use as a parallel to the gap-day goring case?
o: Tum'at met vs. tum'at sheretz
A niddah whose mid-day check was missed (Rav Ada bar Aḥawa vs. Rav Huna)
Mu'ad vs. tam in shor-gores-shor
Mishkav vs. moshav
quiz.e: Rav Ada bar Aḥawa says נִידָּה וַודַּאי; Rav Huna says נִדָּה סָפֵק — same gap-evidence question.

quiz.q: How does R' Yitzchak narrow the rule of אָדָם מוּעָד לְעוֹלָם?
o: Only when both people were already sleeping (שְׁנֵיהֶם יְשֵׁינִין)
Only when fully awake
Only on Shabbat
Only with vessels, not bodily injury
quiz.e: R' Yitzchak: if one came to sleep next to a sleeper, the joining party is the muad — he created the risk.

quiz.q: In the new Mishnah (הַמַּנִּיחַ אֶת הַכַּד), who pays whom?
o: The walker pays for breaking the jar
The jar-owner pays for the walker's injury, and the walker is exempt for the jar
Both pay each other equally
Both are exempt
quiz.e: נִתְקַל בָּהּ וּשְׁבָרָהּ — פָּטוּר. וְאִם הוּזַּק בָּהּ בַּעַל הֶחָבִית חַיָיב בִּנְזָקָיו.
